TypeScript ships with several utility types built on conditional types:
Keep members of T assignable to U
Remove members of T assignable to U
wrap both sides in a tuple:
. This is important when you want to compare union types as a whole rather than member by member.
Conditional types can be nested and chained, like a
statement in the type system. Deep nesting quickly becomes hard to read — prefer named intermediate types when the chain is longer than 2 levels.
uses generics for response typing. Conditional types help you build wrappers that preserve the inferred type through your service layer:

— a conditional type can reference itself in either branch. This enables deep transformations like flattening nested arrays or deeply unwrapping promises. Recursion terminates because the recursed type is always structurally smaller.
Conditional Types with Template Literal Types
enables powerful string manipulation at the type level — something Angular applications use for defining typed event names, route paths, and store action names.
Conditional Types That Resolve to never
Because conditional types resolve at compile time, testing them means verifying type assignments without runtime overhead. The pattern is to use TypeScript's
operator or assertion helpers that produce type errors for wrong types.
selects a type at compile time — no runtime cost.

content: Conditional types distribute over unions when T is a bare type parameter — each union member is checked separately.

content: Built-in utilities like ReturnType, Parameters, Awaited, Extract, and Exclude are all implemented as conditional types.

content: Chain conditionals for type-safe switch-like dispatch; extract named intermediate types to keep chains readable.
In Angular, conditional types power accurate generic service methods, signal value extraction, and route param typing.
